comments.author: @globerealife

comments.author: @GlobeTravel2026
comments.text: Leaving sleeping babies outside cafes in freezing temperatures without fear of crime shows an astonishing level of mutual societal trust. Can a modern, highly populated nation ever build that level of absolute community trust, or is it only possible in a small, isolated society?

comments.author: @telehappy_usa

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text: That’s such an interesting observation! People can adapt to cold weather in surprisingly different ways, and sometimes local fashion habits seem to defy the temperature completely. 😊 Perhaps growing up in a cold climate makes people more comfortable with it.

comments.author: @shaundonovan8816
comments.text: "No Crime" yet Bankers in Jail ???

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text:
When people say Iceland has "no crime," they're usually referring to street crime and violent offences, which are exceptionally rare over there—in fact, regular police officers don't even carry firearms on their daily patrols!
However, when the 2008 financial crash hit, Iceland took a very unique path compared to the rest of the world. Instead of using taxpayer money to bail out the banks, their justice system held financial executives legally accountable for fraud and market manipulation, which resulted in actual prison sentences. So while the streets remain remarkably safe and quiet, they take white-collar crime very seriously!

comments.author: @kylekaechele9016
comments.text: 70% of children born out of wedlock spun as a positive is sick.

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text: In Iceland, society views family structures a bit differently. Relationships, legal protections, and social support systems are set up so that children are equally cared for and supported by both parents and the community, regardless of marital status. The goal of the video was simply to highlight how cultural views on family can vary so widely around the world, rather than to push a specific viewpoint.

comments.author: @ianlewis3023
comments.text: What surprised me the most was the number of sunny days you found to film in. Lovely country, been there twice, but blue skies are rare.

replies.author: @ianlewis3023
replies.text: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries We drove a short way along the dirt road that runs through the centre NE towards Akureyri but after about 10 miles realised we were in a complete wilderness with no facilities if we broke down or got lost and so returned. Saw a pod of Orcas just metres away off a rocky shore in the NW was also great and of course all the traditional sights like Geyser. We also flew to Greenland from Reykjavik for a day trip another amazing place but a day trip was enough.

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text: Iceland’s weather can change incredibly quickly, so we were very lucky to capture so many clear skies. It really is a beautiful country in every kind of weather. Since you’ve been there twice, which place in Iceland impressed you the most?

comments.author: @RealLifeFilesDoc
comments.text: Watching this on a Wednesday night from Phu Tho, Vietnam! 🇻🇳 As a documentary video editor, I am incredibly impressed by the visual pacing here. The beautiful, colorful aerial shot of Reykjavik in image_882a42.jpg paired with the text "A LANGUAGE FROZEN IN TIME" [19:18] is a masterclass in scriptwriting. Highlighting how they literally invent new words from ancient ones (like a computer being a "number prophetess") instead of borrowing foreign words [19:43] is such a brilliant angle on cultural preservation. Fantastic storytelling! 🇮🇸🎥

comments.text: It seems Iceland has realized that marriage is just a religiously derived ideal, and governments around the world have given in by granting it legal status. replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text: Many people in Iceland do seem to place more importance on trust, commitment, and family life than on the formal ceremony itself. At the same time, others still value marriage for personal, cultural, or legal reasons. In the end, the strength of the relationship matters most
